mnemonics.title: ACID Rain
mnemonics.content: Think of 'ACID Rain' - just as acid rain affects everything it touches, a transaction with ACID properties affects the entire database completely. No partial effects!

mnemonics.title: All Children In Danger
mnemonics.content: A - All (Atomicity), C - Children (Consistency), I - In (Isolation), D - Danger (Durability). A fun phrase to remember ACID!

mnemonics.title: Atomic = Atom (Indivisible)
mnemonics.content: Remember: An atom cannot be split. Similarly, an atomic transaction cannot be divided - it's all-or-nothing!

mnemonics.title: Consistency = Code/Rules
mnemonics.content: Just like following traffic rules for consistency, database must follow constraints, triggers, and cascades to maintain valid states.

mnemonics.title: Isolation = Invisible
mnemonics.content: Each transaction should work as if it's alone - its changes are invisible to other transactions until commit.

mnemonics.title: Durability = Diary
mnemonics.content: Once written in a diary, it's permanent. Similarly, committed transactions are permanently saved, even if system crashes.

acronyms.usage: Use this whenever you need to recall the four fundamental properties of a database transaction. Remember: a transaction must satisfy ALL four properties to be considered reliable.
Used when a transaction must be treated as a single unit - either all operations succeed or none do. Think 'all or nothing'.
Used when the database must transform from one valid state to another, respecting all constraints and rules.
Used when concurrent transactions must not interfere with each other - each should appear to execute independently.
Used when committed changes must survive any future system failures - permanently recorded.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a light switch with two states: ON or OFF - no in-between. A transaction is either fully ON (committed) or fully OFF (aborted). Like flipping a coin - heads or tails, nothing in between!
Imagine a weighing scale balancing constraints on one side and data on the other. The scale must always balance - if it tips, consistency is violated. The database must always maintain this balance.
Picture people in separate, soundproof rooms each doing their own work. They don't know what others are doing (concurrent) but the final result is as if they worked one after another (serializable).
Imagine carving your name on a stone tablet - once engraved, it survives rain, wind, and time. Committed data is 'carved' into permanent storage.

Draw a circle: A (start with atomic action) → C (results in consistent state) → I (other transactions can't see intermediate state) → D (finally durability makes it permanent). The circle represents a complete, reliable transaction cycle.

analogies.analogy: Bank ATM Withdrawal: When you withdraw money, two things happen - cash is dispensed AND account is debited. If cash fails to dispense but account is debited, it's wrong. Either BOTH happen (transaction commits) or NEITHER happens (transaction aborts) - all or nothing!

University Grade System: A student cannot have more than 100% marks or negative attendance. If a transaction tries to enter invalid data (like 150% attendance), it's rejected - maintaining consistency like university rules maintain order.

Shared Google Doc: Two users editing the same doc see their own version until they refresh. Each user's changes are 'isolated' - like concurrent transactions, they don't see each other's uncommitted changes until saved/committed.

Exam Answer Sheet Submitted: Once you submit your answer sheet to the invigilator, even if the exam center catches fire later, your answers are safe. Similarly, once committed to the database, data survives hardware failures.

Ordering Food at Restaurant: When you order (transaction begins), many steps happen - taking order, cooking, serving, billing. If anything goes wrong (kitchen closed, item unavailable), the entire order is cancelled (rollback). If everything works, the order is 'committed' and finalized - with durability of your satisfied hunger!
